title: Beeswax botanical pillar candle
subtitle: A pure beeswax pillar candle decorated with pressed dried botanicals on the outside
excerpt: Pure beeswax has a natural honey-vanilla scent that needs no added fragrance. Poured into a pillar mould and decorated with pressed dried lavender and chamomile on the outside, it burns cleanly and brightly. Beeswax contracts as it sets, so a second small top-up pour is usually needed. This candle is suited to dining tables and mantelpieces.
sourceNotes: Pure beeswax pillar candle method: CandleScience beeswax guide (2023). Beeswax melting point 62 to 65°C; pour at 65 to 70°C. Beeswax contracts approximately 1% on cooling; second pour required. Wick: Square Braid #4 or equivalent for 75 mm diameter beeswax pillar. Botanicals applied while wax is still warm and tacky.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 pillar candle (~200 g, 75 mm diameter, ~100 mm tall)
recipe.batchNotes: Pour multiple moulds from one batch. Apply botanicals to each before the wax sets.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Cure for 24 hours before the first burn. Beeswax candles do not require a long cure like soy.
recipe.temperatureNote: Pour at 65 to 70°C. Below 60°C the wax sets before pouring is complete.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Five or more years away from direct heat and sunlight. Beeswax is naturally antimicrobial and stable. The natural scent fades slowly over several years.

glossaryTerms.definition: Beeswax contracts as it cools, often leaving a small sinkhole or dip at the top of the mould. A second small top-up pour fills this before the candle is demoulded.
The first burn of a pillar candle. Allow the melt pool to reach within 5 mm of the edge before extinguishing to set an even burn pattern.
When a candle burns straight down the centre without melting the edges. Caused by extinguishing before the melt pool is wide enough, or by an undersized wick for the candle diameter.

content.text: Pure beeswax burns brighter and longer than soy and carries a natural warm honey scent without any added fragrance. It also contracts as it cools, so expect
and plan for a small top-up pour. Dried botanicals are pressed onto the outside of the candle while the wax is still warm and slightly tacky.

Melt beeswax over a simmering-water bath on low heat only. Never leave melting wax unattended. Keep a kitchen-fire blanket within reach. Beeswax has a higher flash point than soy but will still catch if overheated.

items.prepNote: Pure beeswax pellets or blocks; natural honey-vanilla scent, no fragrance needed
Pre-waxed Square Braid #4 wick for 75 mm beeswax pillar
Dried, for exterior decoration; press on while wax is still tacky

content.text: Thread the wick through the mould base hole and secure with the wick rod across the top. Seal the base hole with mould sealer or a small piece of Blu Tack to prevent leaks. Stand the mould on a flat, heat-safe surface.

Melt beeswax in the pouring pitcher over a simmering-water bath, heating to 70°C on the probe thermometer. Remove from heat and allow to cool slightly to 65 to 67°C. Pour slowly into the mould, leaving 15 mm headspace at the top. As the wax begins to set (about 30 to 40 minutes), it will pull inward from the edges and may form a small sinkhole.
Reheat the remaining wax in the pitcher to 65°C. Pour a small top-up into the sinkhole to level the base. Allow the candle to set fully at room temperature for at least 2 hours before demoulding.

After the initial pour (before the top-up), while the outer surface is still slightly warm and tacky, gently press dried lavender buds and chamomile flowers onto the sides of the candle through the mould opening, or apply directly if using a silicone mould you can peel back. Once fully set, remove the mould sealer, invert, and slide the candle out. Trim the wick to 5 mm.
. Burn for 2 to 3 hours so the melt pool reaches within 5 mm of the candle edge. Extinguishing too early sets a pattern of
that is difficult to recover from. Trim the wick to 5 mm before every burn.
Five or more years away from direct heat and sunlight. Beeswax is naturally stable. Store upright to prevent the pillar deforming in warm conditions.
